page: Amazon Web Services r6idn.large is a memory-optimized instance powered by the AWS Nitro hypervisor. It features a dedicated Intel Xeon 8375C processor with an x86_64 architecture, operating at a clock speed of 3.5 GHz. The hardware configuration provides 2 vCPUs derived from a single physical core with hyper-threading enabled. It is equipped with 16.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz, yielding an 8:1 memory-to-vCPU ratio. This instance includes a bundled 118 GB local NVMe SSD instance store volume and offers a baseline network bandwidth of 3 Gbps.

Benchmark data reveals a distinct performance profile. Single-core performance is moderate, with stress-ng single-core CPU and Geekbench single-core scores falling into the average category. However, multi-core performance is limited, with stress-ng and Geekbench multi-core scores ranking in the bottom 10% due to the single physical core constraint. Memory bandwidth benchmarks, including large-block read and write tests, also register in the bottom 10%. Cryptographic and vector processing capabilities are supported by hardware flags such as AVX-512 and AES, though overall database operations and Redis SET throughput remain low relative to larger instance classes.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the r6idn.large offers an entry-level entry point into the memory-optimized family with dedicated CPU allocation and local NVMe storage. The inclusion of local SSD storage reduces the need for external block storage for temporary data, though the low multi-core performance and memory bandwidth present clear tradeoffs for compute-heavy tasks. This instance is suited for memory-bound workloads with low parallel processing requirements, such as small-scale databases, caching layers, and lightweight enterprise applications requiring a high memory-to-core ratio.
description: Amazon Web Services r6idn.large is a memory-optimized instance featuring 2 vCPUs from a single physical Intel Xeon 8375C core running at 3.5 GHz. It includes 16.0 GB of DDR4 RAM, a 118 GB local NVMe SSD, and a 3 Gbps baseline network speed. Operating on the Nitro hypervisor, this x86_64 instance delivers average single-core CPU performance but exhibits limited multi-core scaling and memory bandwidth, with benchmarks in these areas falling into the bottom 10%. The hardware profile is suited for memory-intensive applications with low parallel processing demands, such as small databases or caching systems.
